en.homeTagline: The Prosperity Gap in Sub-Saharan Africa is at the same level as the world was in the 1980s, making the region roughly four decades behind the global average.

en.title: Building Productive Cities for an Urbanizing World
en.abstract: Cities are the engine of global economic growth and offer greater economic opportunities, diversity in jobs, and more extensive infrastructure than rural areas. Yet, the benefits of urbanization are not automatic. As urban populations and cities continue to expand, strategic investment in urban planning will be essential to ensure cities deliver opportunity for all.
en.citation: Wadhwa, D and Laesser, C. 2026. “Building Productive Cities for an Urbanizing World.” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A F Pirlea, D Wadhwa, D Mahler, U Serajuddin, A Thudt, and M Welch. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
en.inputs: Harris Selod, Benjamin Stewart, Nishant Yonzan
en.homeFactoidText: urban residents live in slums globally
en.homeTagline: Cities are the engine of global economic growth, but strategic investment in urban planning is essential to ensure cities deliver opportunity for all.

en.title: Is gasoline too cheap? The story of  fossil-fuel subsidies
en.abstract: Fossil fuels have been foundational to forming life as we know it today. To keep the cost of fossil fuels stable and affordable, governments often subsidize the price. However, such subsidies also have negative consequences for the economy as well as the environment. This story sheds light on costs and benefits from subsidizing fossil fuels and suggests pathways forward.
en.citation: todo
en.inputs: todo
en.homeFactoidText: TK
en.homeTagline: TK

en.title: Exposure, Vulnerability, and Adaptation to Climate Hazards
en.abstract: Extreme weather events, such as floods, droughts, storms, and heatwaves occur more often and are stronger due to climate change and impact more than half the world’s population. Almost a third of those affected are also in highly vulnerable conditions and must adapt to a changing climate.
en.citation: Thijs Benschop, David Groves, Jonas Helth Lønborg and Ændra Rininsland. 2026. “Exposure, vulnerability and adaptation to climate hazards” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by D. Mahler, A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO. https://www.doi.org/10.60616/
en.credits: Thijs Benschop, Jonas Helth Lønborg, David Groves
en.inputs: Jia Li, Esther Naikal, Minh Cong Nguyen
en.homeFactoidText: people are exposed and vulnerable to climate hazards
en.homeTagline: Climate change intensifies extreme weather events, exposing more than half the global population, a large share of which is also vulnerable and in need of adaptation.

en.title: The Impact of Increasing Aridity and Dryland Expansion on Soils, Humans and Nature
en.abstract: Over the last 30 years, most regions have experienced rising aridity. As a result drylands have expanded considerably, impacting arable lands, agricultural production and livelihoods. Higher aridity also puts pressure on the habitats of plant and animal species. Setting aside protected areas can help drier areas to remain productive ecologically, but protected areas can conflict with agricultural expansion to guarantee global food security.
en.citation: Maarten Lambrechts. 2026. “100 Years of Past, Present and Future Challenges in Tackling Extreme Poverty”  In Atlas of  Global Development 2026, edited by A. F. Pirlea, U. Serajuddin, A. Thudt, D. Wadhwa, and M. Welch. Washington, DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
en.inputs: todo
en.homeFactoidText: TK
en.homeTagline: TK

en.title: Inequalities in Use of and Exposure to Artificial Intelligence
en.abstract: Artificial Intelligence is reshaping the world, but the digital shift is filtering slowly to the global poor. While wealthy nations race ahead, high costs and a lack of digital skills leave billions behind. In poorer countries, the workplace remains mostly untouched by AI, threatening to turn a technological leap into a permanent global divide.
en.citation: Mahler, D G, Tulp, J W, Wang, H and Weber, M. 2026. “Inequalities in AI use and exposure” In
en.credits: Daniel Gerszon Mahler, He Wang,  Michael Weber
en.inputs: Gordon Blackladder, David Castillo Parra, Gabriel Demombynes, Holly Krambeck,
en.homeFactoidText: of people in low-income countries have the digital skills to use AI
en.homeTagline: While high-income nations rapidly integrate AI, stark infrastructural and skills gaps are leaving low-income countries behind.

en.title: The State of Development Data
en.abstract: Without reliable data, the true extent of extreme poverty or gaps in children's health and learning outcomes remains hidden from the people tasked with fixing them. But as the World Bank's Statistical Performance Indicators (SPI) shows, low data availability is not inevitable. Some economies build strong, reliable data systems, even with limited resources.
en.citation: Prinsloo, Z, Stacy, B and Lambrechts, M. 2026. ”Can we measure progress?” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A F Pirlea, D Wadhwa, D Mahler, U Serajuddin, A Thudt, and M Welch. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
en.inputs: Maria Eugenia Genoni and Alaka Holla
en.homeFactoidText: of low- and middle-income economies, the most recent poverty survey is more than five years old.
en.homeTagline: For many economies, the data guiding budgets and regulations are years out of date, leaving millions of people invisible to policy makers.

en.title: Reversing gains: A world with rising hunger
en.abstract: Reversing decades of progress, global hunger is rising again. Escalating conflicts, frequent climate shocks, and fragile food supply chains are driving this reversal, hitting Sub‑Saharan Africa and the Middle East region the hardest.
en.citation: todo
en.inputs: Bo Pieter Johannes Andree, Bryce Charron, Lorena Corso, Sharad Alan Tandon, Sawaid Abbas, Faisal Mueen Qamer
en.homeFactoidText: TK
en.homeTagline: TK

en.title: Past, Present, and Future: 100 Years of  Challenges in Tackling Extreme Poverty
en.abstract: Global extreme poverty has fallen drastically since 1950, but progress has slowed to its weakest pace in decades. Over half of the world's poor are in countries where poverty is increasing, often due to conflict and weak governance. This trend threatens to reverse decades of progress and could cause global poverty to rise.
en.citation: Mahler
en.credits: Daniel Gerszon Mahler and Maarten Lambrechts
en.inputs: Christoph Lakner, Rinku Murgai, Samuel Kofi Tetteh-Baah, Cameron Nadim Haddad, Mario Negre, Rubena Sukaj, Nishant Yonzan
en.homeFactoidText: of the world's extreme poor live in countries where poverty has increased in the past 10 years
en.homeTagline: After 75 years of historic poverty reduction, shifting global dynamics threaten to reverse our progress.

en.title: Skills Are Used and Built on the Job
en.abstract: Education is one of the most powerful drivers of growth and poverty reduction.
en.citation: Stacy, Brian and Christian Lasser. 2026. ”Using and building skills  on the job”. In
en.inputs: Norbert Schady, Joana Silva, Alaka Holla
en.homeFactoidText: low- and lower-middle income countries, learning-adjusted years of schooling dropped between 2010 and 2025
en.homeTagline: Each year of schooling that a worker attains improves productivity and wages.  But learning does not end at school; workers acquire additional skills through on-the-job learning.

en.title: Shaping the Future of Women in the Workplace
en.abstract: Women face persistent barriers when it comes to participating in the labor force. And those who do work are more likely to be in vulnerable employment, earn lower wages, and shoulder the majority of domestic and care responsibilities, highlighting slow and uneven progress toward gender equality at work.
en.citation: Wadhwa, D., Bonfert, A.T., Rininsland, A. and Lambrechts, M. 2026. “Shaping the Future of Women in the Workplace.” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A F Pirlea, D Wadhwa, D Mahler, U Serajuddin, A Thudt, and M Welch. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
en.inputs: Ana Maria Munoz Boudet, Kathleen Beegle
en.homeFactoidText: women are engaged in income-generating work
en.homeTagline: Women face persistent barriers to work and are more likely to be in vulnerable jobs, earn lower wages, and shoulder care burden.

en.title: Tracking Progress in Global Development
en.abstract: After decades of progress, global development has reached a critical phase. Today, progress is taking place at its slowest pace in 75 years. The slowdown spans several areas of development including life expectancy, poverty reduction, education, and women’s empowerment. Explore the data to see how progress is unfolding across countries worldwide.
en.citation: Lambrechts, M, Mahler D G, Thudt, A and Wadhwa, D. 2026. “Global Progress is Grinding to a Halt.” In
en.inputs: Nishant Yonzan, Rossana Tatulli
en.homeFactoidTitle: Progress is the slowest on record in
en.homeFactoidText: of the explored development indicators
en.homeTagline: Explore progress data on key development areas from poverty reduction and life expectancy to education to understand the global progress slowdown.

en.title: Water Quality: A Foundation for Health and Development
en.abstract: Access to safe drinking water is essential for health and development, yet over 2 billion people, mostly in low- and middle-income countries, still lack it. Many remain exposed to contaminated water, which threatens health, livelihoods, and economic progress, underscoring the urgent need to ensure not only access, but also the safety of drinking water.
en.citation: Kashiwase, H, Fujs, T and Tulp, J W. 2026. “Water quality: A foundation for health and development.” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A F Pirlea, D Wadhwa, D Mahler, U Serajuddin, A Thudt, and M Welch. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
en.inputs: Christian Borja-Vega, Pavel Luengas Sierra, Fan Zhang
en.homeFactoidText: of countries with data (48 out of 94) face water quality challenges
en.homeTagline: Access to safe drinking water is essential for health and development, yet over 2 billion people still lack it. Water quality remains the main barrier to safely managed drinking water.

en.title: The Unfinished Digital Revolution: Expanding Internet Access
en.abstract: Internet access has grown at remarkable speed, yet digital opportunity remains deeply unequal. While networks now reach much of the world, millions of people, especially in poorer countries, are still shut out of the digital economy. The reasons go beyond infrastructure, and reflect gaps in affordability, skills, and inclusive policy design.
en.inputs: Sharada Srinivasan, Daniel Boller
en.visuals: Jan Willem Tulp, Maarten Lambrechts, Illustrations: Drew Bardana
en.homeFactoidText: people worldwide do not use the Internet
en.homeTagline: Internet access has grown at remarkable speed since the turn of the century, yet millions of people are shut out of the digital economy, especially in poorer countries.

en.title: Are some countries being left behind?
en.abstract: The global economy is continuously growing and has proven notably resilient despite disruptions such as the COVID-pandemic. However, in some countries GDP per capita has remained stagnant for decades. Furthermore, economic growth does not necessarily translate into improved living conditions for people. Looking at whether people are in employment, education or training can provide additional insights.
en.citation: todo
en.inputs: todo
en.homeFactoidText: TK
en.homeTagline: TK

en.title: Access to Electricity: Who Remains Without Power?
en.abstract: Global electricity access has expanded steadily over the past two decades, yet progress remains deeply uneven. While most regions have achieved near-universal coverage, Sub-Saharan Africa continues to lag significantly, particularly its rural communities. Initiatives like Mission 300 aim to extend electricity to 300 million more people in Africa by 2030.
en.citation: Fujs, T, Kashiwase, H and Lambrechts, M. 2026. “Access to Electricity: Who Remains Without Power?” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A F Pirlea, D Wadhwa, D Mahler, U Serajuddin, A Thudt, and M Welch. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
en.inputs: Sandeep Kohli, Patrick Rugwizangoga
en.homeFactoidText: people in Sub-Saharan Africa live without electricity today
en.homeTagline: Although global electricity access has expanded steadily over the past two decades, millions in rural Sub-Saharan Africa have been left behind.
